item.title: Wheat Field with Cypresses
item.museum: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
item.lesson: Painted the same extraordinary summer as The Starry Night, this is its daylight twin — the same wind moving through everything, now under a Provençal sun. Van Gogh had become fixated on cypresses, writing to Theo: 'It is as beautiful of line and proportion as an Egyptian obelisk. And the green has a quality of such distinction... a splash of black in a sunny landscape.' Here the dark flame of the trees anchors a field in full churn: wheat like brushed gold, olive trees writhing silver, mountains rolling like surf, and clouds coiled into the same living turbulence he gave the stars. He considered it one of his best summer canvases and repeated it twice for his mother and sister. Where Starry Night unsettles, this one exults — proof that the famous swirling stroke was not simply illness transcribed but a language he could speak in any key, including joy.
item.lookFor: Compare the three greens van Gogh described in his letter: the near-black cypress, the gray-green olives, the warm field — a lesson in how many colors 'green' contains.
